204dc6afe1a6.title: Guideline for Deployment of Machine Learning Models for Predictive Quality in Production
204dc6afe1a6.abstract:
Predicting product quality represents a common area of application of machine learning (ML) in manufacturing. However, manifold challenges
occur during the integration of ML models into production processes. Therefore, this paper aims to provide a guideline for the deployment of ML
models in production environments. Relevant decisions and steps for deploying models in predictive quality use cases are demonstrated. The
results for each component of the proposed guideline – deployment design, productionizing & testing, monitoring, and retraining – have been
validated with industry experts including exemplary implementations.
This is a resupply of March 2023 as the template used in the publication of the original article contained errors. The content of the article has remained unaffected.
816
Henrik Heymann  et al. / Procedia CIRP 107 (2022) 815–820
204dc6afe1a6.full_text:
Available online at www.sciencedirect.com
ScienceDirect
55th CIRP Conference on Manufacturing Systems
Guideline for Deployment of Machine Learning Models for Predictive
Quality in Production
Henrik Heymanna*, Alexander D. Kiesa, Maik Fryea, Robert H. Schmitta,b, Andrés Bozac
aFraunhofer Institute for Production Technology IPT, Steinbachstr. 17, Aachen 52074, Germany
bLaboratory for Machine Tools and Production Engineering WZL of RWTH Aachen University, Campus-Boulevard 30, Aachen 52074, Germany
cCentro de Investigación Gestión e Ingeniería de la Producción (CIGIP), Universitat Politècnica de València, Camino de Vera s/n, Valencia 46022, Spain
Abstract
Predicting product quality represents a common area of application of machine learning (ML) in manufacturing. However, manifold challenges
occur during the integration of ML models into production processes. Therefore, this paper aims to provide a guideline for the deployment of ML
models in production environments. Relevant decisions and steps for deploying models in predictive quality use cases are demonstrated. The
results for each component of the proposed guideline – deployment design, productionizing & testing, monitoring, and retraining – have been
validated with industry experts including exemplary implementations.
© 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0)
Peer-review under responsibility of the International Programme committee of the 55th CIRP Conference on Manufacturing Systems
Keywords: Artificial Intelligence; Machine Learning; Deployment; Production; Manufacturing; Predictive Quality
1. Introduction
In a globalized world, companies from industrialized nations
must be able to manufacture products of superior quality at
competitive costs [1]. One way of achieving the highest pos-
sible level of quality is using ML to analyze past defects in
production and predict future events to derive remedial
measures [2]. ML as a subdomain of artificial intelligence (AI)
is an emerging subject in investigation with increasing interest
from the industry [3].
Deployment is necessary for making use of performant ML
models in production. Studies and surveys from practice show
that a high percentage of ML projects fail without a successful
deployment[4]. As a consequence of unsuccessful deploy-
ments, companies waste a substantial amount of resources [5].
Due to numerous technological but also organizational
challenges, deploying ML models into the running production
This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0)
Peer-review under responsibility of the International Programme committee of the 55th CIRP Conference on Manufacturing Systems
10.1016/j.procir.2022.05.068
www.elsevier.com/locate/procedia
process proves to be enormously difficult [6]. From a techno-
logical point of view, a main challenge consists in the
constantly changing landscape of software tools for data and AI
applications [7]. At the same time, the support and under-
standing by decision owners as well as the alignment of ML
with the business problem are crucial aspects to overcome the
organizational barriers for deployment [8].
This paper aims to provide a framework for the ML model
deployment applied to the context of predictive quality in
production. A comprehensive guideline is developed to
facilitate the successful integration of ML models for predictive
quality. On the one hand, it can be applied to a wide range of
scenarios due to its generic approach. On the other hand, the
concept considers the individual requirements of predictive
quality use cases by providing relevant decisions and steps. The
deployment is broken down in a structured and target-oriented
manner to provide the best value for decision owners with
background
in business or production with no expert
knowledge in software or ML.
The remainder of this paper is structured as follows. Section
2 describes the procedure of the literature review and presents
the key findings. In section 3, the methodology for the guideline
development is presented followed by the results in section 4.
How the guideline can be applied to a predictive quality use
case in the production context is described in section 5. Section
6 provides concluding remarks.
2. Literature Review
Within the Cross Industry Standard Process for Data Mining
(CRISP-DM), deployment is understood as the integration of
the ML model into an organization’s decision-making
processes [9]. In this paper, the focus is set on deploying ML
models for predictive quality. Predictive quality is defined as
“the empowerment of the user to optimize product and process-
related quality by using data-driven predictions as a basis for
decision-making and action” [10]. For the literature review,
publications are accumulated, out of which the most relevant
ones are then selected. Subsequently, the selected publications
are categorized and evaluated.
In accordance with the fact that the topic of deployment of
ML models for predictive quality in production is located on
the verge of multiple fields of investigation, search strings from
ML, production and software engineering are used in
combination. Gray literature is also considered for the search
as academic literature only gives an incomplete view on
dynamic fields of investigation [11]. Publications by practi-
tioners represent valuable sources of information and may have
high relevance for the topic. Nonetheless, they require a
profound analysis of worthiness before being selected.
A categorization allows classifying the selected items based
on their origin either in production engineering or software
engineering. Subsequently, selected approaches are evaluated
by means of four criteria illustrated in Fig. 1.

As an excerpt of the overall evaluation results, three relevant
publications are highlighted and presented in more detail at this
point. Schmitt J et al. [14] provide a new integrated solution of
predictive model-based quality inspection in industrial
manufacturing using ML techniques and Edge Cloud
Computing
technology.
In
the
provided
framework,
deployment and the technical implementation into an existing
IT plant infrastructure
are covered from a high-level
perspective. Even though transferability is given, the approach
does not point out different deployment options.
Sato et al. [18] introduce the concept of Continuous
Delivery for Machine Learning, which brings continuous
delivery principles and practices to ML applications. In the
form of a guideline, an end-to-end lifecycle from model
building to deployment and monitoring is presented. Due to the
high level of detail provided by the well-respected
practitioners, the approach is very frequently cited within the
community. However, no connection to industrial production
or quality-related applications in manufacturing is established.
John et al. [22] conduct a systematic literature review of the
state of the art and practice of architecting AI deployment. A
framework about how to integrate, deploy, operationalize, and
evolve AI models is derived for the end-to-end deployment
process. Relevant practices and challenges are extracted from
literature and compared creating a comprehensive overview of
necessary, well-transferable tasks. Given its origin in software,
the approach does not address characteristics of predictive
quality use cases in production environments.
Table 1 and the preceding exposition show that no existing
approach fulfills all criteria sufficiently and the overall goal of
the paper is not met. Therefore, further research is necessary.
3. Methodology
Based on the identified research gap, a guideline for the
deployment of ML models for predictive quality is developed.
In prior research regarding ML pipelines in production, the
blocks data integration, data preparation, modeling and
deployment are defined [12, 23]. In the proposed guideline, the
deployment is divided into deployment design, productionizing
& testing, monitoring, and retraining. There are dependencies
between the components in form of sequential execution and
feedback loops. Since the deployment design defines the
system’s design requirements, it serves as an input for the
subsequent steps and remains unchanged during the process
(Fig. 2).
Deployment
Productionizing
Monitoring
Retraining
Design
& Testing
Fig. 2. Deployment guideline components.
For each component, necessary decisions and steps for a
successful deployment
are identified based on existing
approaches from theory paired with insights from the practical
validation. Methods used for validation are described in a
subsequent section. To develop the guideline, concepts from
different sources are aligned and brought together in a clear
structure. While doing so, a strong focus is set on predictive
quality applications.
At the end of the development process, there is a
comprehensive guideline for deploying ML models in
predictive quality use cases. Nonetheless, the underlying
structure of the developed procedure is sufficiently generic so
that it is extendable to further use cases such as predictive
maintenance or anomaly detection. In alignment with the
overall objective of the paper, the detailed description of
decisions and steps ensures a systematic approach during
deployment.
4. Results
In the following, each guideline component is explained
concerning the application in predictive quality. Furthermore,
collaboration and tool selection are covered as overarching
aspects relevant for fruitful deployment efforts.
4.1. Deployment design
As the goal of the deployment design, requirements towards
the ML deployment system architecture are determined. For
this purpose, five design parameters in conjunction with the
respective options are identified (Fig. 3).
Prediction approach
How are predictions made?
Consuming application
How are predictions consumed?
Model serving
How are models served?
Learning method
How are models updated?
Hosting solution
How is the system hosted?
Fig. 3. Deployment design parameters.
By choosing one value for each parameter in form of a
morphological box, existing architectures can be replicated,
and new, unseen ones can be created based on the use case
characteristics. Applying the concept of batch and streaming
processing to predictive quality, predictions about the product
quality can be calculated on batches of input data or in real time
[13]. Time critical applications such as real-time parameter
adjustments are treated differently to scenarios where there is a
time buffer between the generation of input data and the use of
the model’s outputs. Predictions can be consumed in a web app,
which can be accessed in the browser or by other services to
retrieve the quality predictions for purposes of further analysis
such as dashboards. Alternatively, a native app is installed on a
smart device or integrated into the machine control software to
process the predictions. The model can be embedded into the
consuming application in a monolith architecture or served as
a separate service to achieve a microservice architecture. In the
case of a separate model serving, the prediction results delivery
can be realized via database, REST API or streaming [18, 22].
In accordance with the modeling phase before the deployment,
the model can learn offline on batches of training data or online
with every new data point [24]. Options for hosting the overall
system include on-premises such as in-device or on-site as well
as in a public or private cloud [21]. Which option to select for
each parameter depends on the circumstances of every
individual case. Existing boundary
conditions
heavily
influence the decision. Furthermore, effects on the complexity
and scalability of the target system must be considered.
4.2. Productionizing & testing
Productionizing is understood as a series of implementation
tasks. Testing ensures reproducibility during an ML model’s
transfer from the research environment to a production
environment. In this context, the term production is used
ambiguously for the final stage of software development as
well as for the industrial production as in manufacturing and
assembly. This component aims to develop a deployable
application around the ML model. As a result, this leads to a
process that is similar to regular software development (Fig. 4).
Plan
What are the application requirements?
Develop
How is the application written?
Test
How is the application tested?
Release
How is the application released?
Fig. 4. Productionizing & testing process.
Nonetheless, in the case of ML the process is more complex
than for traditional software. In addition to the code, there are
two more ML-specific dimensions in form of data and model
[18]. This operational process is generally valid and not limited
to predictive quality use cases but represents one of the most
time-consuming activities in deployment.
First, the required ML functionality is to be planned, that the
application needs to fulfill in an isolated manner or in
interaction with other systems. On this basis, the code for the
application is written including the transfer of modeling code,
e.g., provided by a data scientist in Jupyter Notebook, into the
target programming language and creating a serialized object
of the ML model respectively multiple models for further use.
The deployment of ML models does not only refer to bringing
an ML algorithm into production but includes the whole
pipeline containing data preparation steps plus the algorithm
[25]. In addition to the code itself, training data and resulting
models need to be versioned and tracked. Operational and ML-
related tests are essential to ensure that only well-performing
ML model applications are rolled out to the users. Different
release strategies exist to safely replace a running application
with a newer version. To increase speed and reliability of the
productionizing & testing process, the phases can be automated
in the sense of Continuous Delivery [18].
4.3. Monitoring
Once in production, the ML model application is monitored
on different levels. In this guideline, the monitoring is analyzed
from an ML perspective rather than from an operational point
of view (Fig. 5).
Understand
What is the system doing?
Monitor
Is the system working?
Analyze
How to improve the system?
Fig. 5. Monitoring levels.
Metrics, logs and traces are called the three pillars of
observability and enable to understand the behavior of a system
for predictive quality [26]. The same metrics as in the modeling
phase are applied for the monitoring. In the case of binary
classification tasks, e.g., classifying a product’s quality as ok
or not ok, four metrics are relevant: accuracy, precision, recall
and F1 score [2]. A model’s predictions can be analyzed later
by logging input data from production and the predicted
quality. If multiple services are involved with handling a
prediction request, traces represent a method to follow the
request through the system. In the case of microservice
architectures, tracing allows to determine exactly where a delay
or an error occurred, e.g.,
in
data fetching
or
data
pre-processing.
All gathered monitoring information can be displayed in
dashboards as a high-level view for the management. In
predictive quality, such dashboards combine two kinds of
information. On the one hand, they allow process monitoring
to track the product quality predicted by the ML model. On the
other hand, they are used for monitoring the performance of the
ML model application itself. Alerts are directly addressed
notifications for the responsible entity based on predefined
criteria such as intervention and warning limits for metrics. On
top of that, a root cause analysis is executed to derive adequate
measures. Possible root causes include hardware errors such as
broken sensors as well as ML-independent software errors such
as code bugs or unavailability of services. In the case of ML
model performance issues, a separate analysis aims to detect
drifts that lead to a to deteriorating model performance [27]. A
drift may cause the same sensor values to not lead to the same
product quality in predictive quality applications. This can be
caused by effects such as tool wear, that are not considered by
the ML model.
4.4. Retraining
Retraining of ML models becomes inevitable in order to
maintain a high performance [28]. Three interconnected
decisions are identified as relevant for the retraining (Fig. 6).
Trigger
When is the retraining triggered?
Extent
How extensive is the retraining?
Execution
How is the retraining executed?
Fig. 6. Retraining decisions.
Periodic triggering of the retraining based on a fixed
schedule has the disadvantage of inefficient use of resources
[29]. Alternatively, a model can be retrained on demand based
on defined criteria. In predictive quality use cases, notifications
from monitoring about performance measures falling below a
warning or intervention limit facilitate targeted actions. Based
on the monitoring data, the extent of the retraining is
determined [30]. Retraining in the narrower sense consists of
training a model on a new data set. If this does not achieve the
desired performance, a redesign of the model is necessary. In
that case, a
new model pipeline with different data
preprocessing steps is set up and trained. Whereas in the
retraining only an adjustment of a model’s weights is realized
e.g., by increasing the feature importance of a particular sensor
value, redesign involves adding or removing data sources or
sensors, selecting ML algorithms and tuning hyperparameters.
Retraining is closely connected to the initial model building
and, thus, all decisions must be aligned with the modeling
execution. Approaches to reduce the human involvement in the
training and retraining through automated ML (AutoML) must
be analyzed for every specific given case [28].
Retraining is the last component of the guideline but does
not mark the end of the deployment. Rather, productionizing &
testing, monitoring, and retraining are understood as a cycle of
continuously performed tasks.
4.5. Collaboration and tool selection
Overarching aspects for deploying ML models are the
assurance of collaboration and selection of tools. From an
organizational point of view, the success of deployment
depends on the alignment of three types of expertise in the
project: process expertise, data science expertise and DevOps
expertise [22]. The inclusion and support of stakeholders from
the business perspective of ML is crucial and can be enhanced
through explainability techniques [19]. Comprehending why an
ML model assigned a quality label to a product increases the
user’s trust in the prediction and even allows a better
understanding of the process by experts.
The selection of adequate tools poses a critical technological
challenge. First, a decision between open source tools and
proprietary solutions is required and is followed by the
selection of a particular product. Relevant evaluation criteria
consist in functional, technical, organizational, legal,
economical, and political requirements [31]. The weights of the
criteria are adjusted individually in alignment with the use case
and the company’s characteristics. Regarding companies in the
manufacturing industry
planning to implement ML for
predictive quality, the final selection can vary strongly between
small or medium sized enterprises in comparison to large
corporations.
5. Validation
Validating conceptual models consists in asking if the
developed model contains all necessary details to achieve the
study’s goals [32]. To ensure that the proposed guideline
satisfies its intended use and user needs, it was validated by
means of a consortium study with industry partners. Within the
International Center for Networked and Adaptive Production
(ICNAP) three Aachen-based Fraunhofer Institutes and
renowned industrial partners are developing solutions for
production systems and value chains aimed at Industry 4.0.
Validation was achieved through interviews with experts and
an exemplary implementation of a predictive quality use case.
5.1. Expert interviews
Input and feedback of practitioners are valuable sources for
the refinement of the guideline. On the one hand, interviews
with deployment experts were conducted. All three interviewed
experts have a background in research and had deployed ML
models in past research and industry projects. As a result, the
interviews show that previously realized deployments provided
by the experts are covered by the guideline. Additionally, the
guideline enables the realization of more efficient deployments
in the future by introducing new approaches from the literature
review, which have not been fully adapted in the practical
deployment yet.
On the other hand, all components were validated in
workshops within the ICNAP study through discussions with
experts from a corporate background. In each one of the
multiple workshops, the guideline was discussed with up to six
experts. Whereas all the involved companies operate in the
production industry, their sizes range from medium to large
enterprises. Likewise, the participants’ areas of work vary
including the fields of data science, software, and process
monitoring. Due to the wide ranges of competences the focus
was set on validating the completeness and understandability
of the guideline. Based on the experts’ feedback, the orientation
and elaboration of the guideline were adjusted to the expressed
needs.
5.2. Hands-on implementation
Based on the realistic requirements provided by the ICNAP
community members, an existing trained ML model was
deployed following the guideline from deployment design,
productionizing & testing, monitoring, and retraining. A
publicly available real-life data set from semiconductor
manufacturing provided by the University of California Irvine
[33] was used for the initial training. In the use case, instances
are classified as pass or fail based on sensor data collected
during the process. The deployed ML model application
includes prediction, monitoring, and retraining functionality.
Wrapped into a Flask backend, the model returns predictions
based on requests of data in JSON format. Furthermore,
available model versions can be retrieved, monitoring data in
form performance metrics or a confusion matrix can be
calculated, and new model versions can be trained on request.
Using the software Postman, it is possible to make requests to
the application’s endpoints and visualize responses. Especially
the practical implementation could ensure the guideline’s
applicability for the user.
6. Conclusion
In this paper, a guideline for deploying ML models in
production
environments
was
developed.
It
supports
companies to fully reap the benefits of modeling efforts by
deploying built models into production and using their results,
where they create added value. Given the importance of
ensuring successful deployments,
a clearly structured
methodology was lacking. Therefore, the deployment was
broken down into separate guideline components. For each
component, relevant decisions and steps were provided in a
high-level, generally valid form. Based on this, it was described
in detail how introduced decisions and steps can be applied to
predictive quality under consideration of individual needs and
restrictions of any specific use case.
Limitations exist regarding the area of application. The
guideline
was
conceptualized
for
predictive
quality
applications in production. In its current form, it cannot address
all deviating scenarios with different requirements towards the
deployment. In this context, the validation was also conducted
with a limited scope.
Due to the relevance of deployment across industries, future
lines of investigation include an analysis of how to adjust the
guideline to further use cases within or beyond the
manufacturing industry. Furthermore, the integration of
DevOps techniques into the ML life cycle with the ultimate
goal of automating the whole end-to-end ML pipeline from
data integration to deployment is part of future research.

Keywords: Artificial Intelligence; Machine Learning; Deployment; Production; Manufacturing; Predictive Quality

In a globalized world, companies from industrialized nations
must be able to manufacture products of superior quality at
competitive costs [1]. One way of achieving the highest pos-
sible level of quality is using ML to analyze past defects in
production and predict future events to derive remedial
measures [2]. ML as a subdomain of artificial intelligence (AI)
is an emerging subject in investigation with increasing interest
from the industry [3].
Deployment is necessary for making use of performant ML
models in production. Studies and surveys from practice show
that a high percentage of ML projects fail without a successful
deployment[4]. As a consequence of unsuccessful deploy-
ments, companies waste a substantial amount of resources [5].
Due to numerous technological but also organizational
challenges, deploying ML models into the running production
process proves to be enormously difficult [6]. From a techno-
logical point of view, a main challenge consists in the
constantly changing landscape of software tools for data and AI
applications [7]. At the same time, the support and under-
standing by decision owners as well as the alignment of ML
with the business problem are crucial aspects to overcome the
organizational barriers for deployment [8].
This paper aims to provide a framework for the ML model
deployment applied to the context of predictive quality in
production. A comprehensive guideline is developed to
facilitate the successful integration of ML models for predictive
quality. On the one hand, it can be applied to a wide range of
scenarios due to its generic approach. On the other hand, the
concept considers the individual requirements of predictive
quality use cases by providing relevant decisions and steps. The
deployment is broken down in a structured and target-oriented
manner to provide the best value for decision owners with

Guideline for Deployment of Machine Learning Models for Predictive
Quality in Production

Predicting product quality represents a common area of application of machine learning (ML) in manufacturing. However, manifold challenges
occur during the integration of ML models into production processes. Therefore, this paper aims to provide a guideline for the deployment of ML
models in production environments. Relevant decisions and steps for deploying models in predictive quality use cases are demonstrated. The
results for each component of the proposed guideline – deployment design, productionizing & testing, monitoring, and retraining – have been
validated with industry experts including exemplary implementations.

The remainder of this paper is structured as follows. Section
2 describes the procedure of the literature review and presents
the key findings. In section 3, the methodology for the guideline
development is presented followed by the results in section 4.
How the guideline can be applied to a predictive quality use
case in the production context is described in section 5. Section
6 provides concluding remarks.

Within the Cross Industry Standard Process for Data Mining
(CRISP-DM), deployment is understood as the integration of
the ML model into an organization’s decision-making
processes [9]. In this paper, the focus is set on deploying ML
models for predictive quality. Predictive quality is defined as
“the empowerment of the user to optimize product and process-
related quality by using data-driven predictions as a basis for
decision-making and action” [10]. For the literature review,
publications are accumulated, out of which the most relevant
ones are then selected. Subsequently, the selected publications
are categorized and evaluated.
In accordance with the fact that the topic of deployment of
ML models for predictive quality in production is located on
the verge of multiple fields of investigation, search strings from
ML, production and software engineering are used in
combination. Gray literature is also considered for the search
as academic literature only gives an incomplete view on
dynamic fields of investigation [11]. Publications by practi-
tioners represent valuable sources of information and may have
high relevance for the topic. Nonetheless, they require a
profound analysis of worthiness before being selected.
A categorization allows classifying the selected items based
on their origin either in production engineering or software
engineering. Subsequently, selected approaches are evaluated
by means of four criteria illustrated in Fig. 1.
Fig. 1. Evaluation criteria.
First, it is analyzed how detailed the use of ML for predictive
quality (criterion 1) is treated. Similarly, criterion 2 evaluates
the covered depth of the topic deployment of ML. Furthermore,
it is assessed to which degree a guideline structure is followed
(criterion 3). The fourth and last criterion evaluates the
transferability of presented concepts to other use cases
(criterion 4). Table 1 gives an overview of the categorization
and evaluation of existing approaches by means of the
introduced set of criteria.

As an excerpt of the overall evaluation results, three relevant
publications are highlighted and presented in more detail at this
point. Schmitt J et al. [14] provide a new integrated solution of
predictive model-based quality inspection in industrial
manufacturing using ML techniques and Edge Cloud
Computing
technology.
In
the
provided
framework,
deployment and the technical implementation into an existing
IT plant infrastructure
are covered from a high-level
perspective. Even though transferability is given, the approach
does not point out different deployment options.
Sato et al. [18] introduce the concept of Continuous
Delivery for Machine Learning, which brings continuous
delivery principles and practices to ML applications. In the
form of a guideline, an end-to-end lifecycle from model
building to deployment and monitoring is presented. Due to the
high level of detail provided by the well-respected
practitioners, the approach is very frequently cited within the
community. However, no connection to industrial production
or quality-related applications in manufacturing is established.
John et al. [22] conduct a systematic literature review of the
state of the art and practice of architecting AI deployment. A
framework about how to integrate, deploy, operationalize, and
evolve AI models is derived for the end-to-end deployment
process. Relevant practices and challenges are extracted from
literature and compared creating a comprehensive overview of
necessary, well-transferable tasks. Given its origin in software,
the approach does not address characteristics of predictive
quality use cases in production environments.
Table 1 and the preceding exposition show that no existing
approach fulfills all criteria sufficiently and the overall goal of
the paper is not met. Therefore, further research is necessary.

Based on the identified research gap, a guideline for the
deployment of ML models for predictive quality is developed.
In prior research regarding ML pipelines in production, the
blocks data integration, data preparation, modeling and

deployment are defined [12, 23]. In the proposed guideline, the
deployment is divided into deployment design, productionizing
& testing, monitoring, and retraining. There are dependencies
between the components in form of sequential execution and
feedback loops. Since the deployment design defines the
system’s design requirements, it serves as an input for the
subsequent steps and remains unchanged during the process
(Fig. 2).
Fig. 2. Deployment guideline components.
For each component, necessary decisions and steps for a
successful deployment
are identified based on existing
approaches from theory paired with insights from the practical
validation. Methods used for validation are described in a
subsequent section. To develop the guideline, concepts from
different sources are aligned and brought together in a clear
structure. While doing so, a strong focus is set on predictive
quality applications.
At the end of the development process, there is a
comprehensive guideline for deploying ML models in
predictive quality use cases. Nonetheless, the underlying
structure of the developed procedure is sufficiently generic so
that it is extendable to further use cases such as predictive
maintenance or anomaly detection. In alignment with the
overall objective of the paper, the detailed description of
decisions and steps ensures a systematic approach during
deployment.

In the following, each guideline component is explained
concerning the application in predictive quality. Furthermore,
collaboration and tool selection are covered as overarching
aspects relevant for fruitful deployment efforts.
4.1. Deployment design
As the goal of the deployment design, requirements towards
the ML deployment system architecture are determined. For
this purpose, five design parameters in conjunction with the
respective options are identified (Fig. 3).
Fig. 3. Deployment design parameters.
By choosing one value for each parameter in form of a
morphological box, existing architectures can be replicated,
and new, unseen ones can be created based on the use case
characteristics. Applying the concept of batch and streaming
processing to predictive quality, predictions about the product
quality can be calculated on batches of input data or in real time
[13]. Time critical applications such as real-time parameter
adjustments are treated differently to scenarios where there is a
time buffer between the generation of input data and the use of
the model’s outputs. Predictions can be consumed in a web app,
which can be accessed in the browser or by other services to
retrieve the quality predictions for purposes of further analysis
such as dashboards. Alternatively, a native app is installed on a
smart device or integrated into the machine control software to
process the predictions. The model can be embedded into the
consuming application in a monolith architecture or served as
a separate service to achieve a microservice architecture. In the
case of a separate model serving, the prediction results delivery
can be realized via database, REST API or streaming [18, 22].
In accordance with the modeling phase before the deployment,
the model can learn offline on batches of training data or online
with every new data point [24]. Options for hosting the overall
system include on-premises such as in-device or on-site as well
as in a public or private cloud [21]. Which option to select for
each parameter depends on the circumstances of every
individual case. Existing boundary
conditions
heavily
influence the decision. Furthermore, effects on the complexity
and scalability of the target system must be considered.
4.2. Productionizing & testing
Productionizing is understood as a series of implementation
tasks. Testing ensures reproducibility during an ML model’s
transfer from the research environment to a production
environment. In this context, the term production is used
ambiguously for the final stage of software development as
well as for the industrial production as in manufacturing and
assembly. This component aims to develop a deployable
application around the ML model. As a result, this leads to a
process that is similar to regular software development (Fig. 4).
Fig. 4. Productionizing & testing process.
Nonetheless, in the case of ML the process is more complex
than for traditional software. In addition to the code, there are
two more ML-specific dimensions in form of data and model
[18]. This operational process is generally valid and not limited
to predictive quality use cases but represents one of the most
time-consuming activities in deployment.

First, the required ML functionality is to be planned, that the
application needs to fulfill in an isolated manner or in
interaction with other systems. On this basis, the code for the
application is written including the transfer of modeling code,
e.g., provided by a data scientist in Jupyter Notebook, into the
target programming language and creating a serialized object
of the ML model respectively multiple models for further use.
The deployment of ML models does not only refer to bringing
an ML algorithm into production but includes the whole
pipeline containing data preparation steps plus the algorithm
[25]. In addition to the code itself, training data and resulting
models need to be versioned and tracked. Operational and ML-
related tests are essential to ensure that only well-performing
ML model applications are rolled out to the users. Different
release strategies exist to safely replace a running application
with a newer version. To increase speed and reliability of the
productionizing & testing process, the phases can be automated
in the sense of Continuous Delivery [18].
4.3. Monitoring
Once in production, the ML model application is monitored
on different levels. In this guideline, the monitoring is analyzed
from an ML perspective rather than from an operational point
of view (Fig. 5).
Fig. 5. Monitoring levels.
Metrics, logs and traces are called the three pillars of
observability and enable to understand the behavior of a system
for predictive quality [26]. The same metrics as in the modeling
phase are applied for the monitoring. In the case of binary
classification tasks, e.g., classifying a product’s quality as ok
or not ok, four metrics are relevant: accuracy, precision, recall
and F1 score [2]. A model’s predictions can be analyzed later
by logging input data from production and the predicted
quality. If multiple services are involved with handling a
prediction request, traces represent a method to follow the
request through the system. In the case of microservice
architectures, tracing allows to determine exactly where a delay
or an error occurred, e.g.,
in
data fetching
or

pre-processing.
All gathered monitoring information can be displayed in
dashboards as a high-level view for the management. In
predictive quality, such dashboards combine two kinds of
information. On the one hand, they allow process monitoring
to track the product quality predicted by the ML model. On the
other hand, they are used for monitoring the performance of the
ML model application itself. Alerts are directly addressed
notifications for the responsible entity based on predefined
criteria such as intervention and warning limits for metrics. On
top of that, a root cause analysis is executed to derive adequate
measures. Possible root causes include hardware errors such as
broken sensors as well as ML-independent software errors such
as code bugs or unavailability of services. In the case of ML
model performance issues, a separate analysis aims to detect
drifts that lead to a to deteriorating model performance [27]. A
drift may cause the same sensor values to not lead to the same
product quality in predictive quality applications. This can be
caused by effects such as tool wear, that are not considered by
the ML model.
4.4. Retraining
Retraining of ML models becomes inevitable in order to
maintain a high performance [28]. Three interconnected
decisions are identified as relevant for the retraining (Fig. 6).
Fig. 6. Retraining decisions.
Periodic triggering of the retraining based on a fixed
schedule has the disadvantage of inefficient use of resources
[29]. Alternatively, a model can be retrained on demand based
on defined criteria. In predictive quality use cases, notifications
from monitoring about performance measures falling below a
warning or intervention limit facilitate targeted actions. Based
on the monitoring data, the extent of the retraining is
determined [30]. Retraining in the narrower sense consists of
training a model on a new data set. If this does not achieve the
desired performance, a redesign of the model is necessary. In
that case, a
new model pipeline with different data
preprocessing steps is set up and trained. Whereas in the
retraining only an adjustment of a model’s weights is realized
e.g., by increasing the feature importance of a particular sensor
value, redesign involves adding or removing data sources or
sensors, selecting ML algorithms and tuning hyperparameters.
Retraining is closely connected to the initial model building
and, thus, all decisions must be aligned with the modeling
execution. Approaches to reduce the human involvement in the
training and retraining through automated ML (AutoML) must
be analyzed for every specific given case [28].
Retraining is the last component of the guideline but does
not mark the end of the deployment. Rather, productionizing &
testing, monitoring, and retraining are understood as a cycle of
continuously performed tasks.
4.5. Collaboration and tool selection
Overarching aspects for deploying ML models are the
assurance of collaboration and selection of tools. From an
organizational point of view, the success of deployment
depends on the alignment of three types of expertise in the
project: process expertise, data science expertise and DevOps
expertise [22]. The inclusion and support of stakeholders from
the business perspective of ML is crucial and can be enhanced

through explainability techniques [19]. Comprehending why an
ML model assigned a quality label to a product increases the
user’s trust in the prediction and even allows a better
understanding of the process by experts.
The selection of adequate tools poses a critical technological
challenge. First, a decision between open source tools and
proprietary solutions is required and is followed by the
selection of a particular product. Relevant evaluation criteria
consist in functional, technical, organizational, legal,
economical, and political requirements [31]. The weights of the
criteria are adjusted individually in alignment with the use case
and the company’s characteristics. Regarding companies in the
manufacturing industry
planning to implement ML for
predictive quality, the final selection can vary strongly between
small or medium sized enterprises in comparison to large
corporations.

Validating conceptual models consists in asking if the
developed model contains all necessary details to achieve the
study’s goals [32]. To ensure that the proposed guideline
satisfies its intended use and user needs, it was validated by
means of a consortium study with industry partners. Within the
International Center for Networked and Adaptive Production
(ICNAP) three Aachen-based Fraunhofer Institutes and
renowned industrial partners are developing solutions for
production systems and value chains aimed at Industry 4.0.
Validation was achieved through interviews with experts and
an exemplary implementation of a predictive quality use case.
5.1. Expert interviews
Input and feedback of practitioners are valuable sources for
the refinement of the guideline. On the one hand, interviews
with deployment experts were conducted. All three interviewed
experts have a background in research and had deployed ML
models in past research and industry projects. As a result, the
interviews show that previously realized deployments provided
by the experts are covered by the guideline. Additionally, the
guideline enables the realization of more efficient deployments
in the future by introducing new approaches from the literature
review, which have not been fully adapted in the practical
deployment yet.
On the other hand, all components were validated in
workshops within the ICNAP study through discussions with
experts from a corporate background. In each one of the
multiple workshops, the guideline was discussed with up to six
experts. Whereas all the involved companies operate in the
production industry, their sizes range from medium to large
enterprises. Likewise, the participants’ areas of work vary
including the fields of data science, software, and process
monitoring. Due to the wide ranges of competences the focus
was set on validating the completeness and understandability
of the guideline. Based on the experts’ feedback, the orientation
and elaboration of the guideline were adjusted to the expressed
needs.
5.2. Hands-on implementation
Based on the realistic requirements provided by the ICNAP
community members, an existing trained ML model was
deployed following the guideline from deployment design,
productionizing & testing, monitoring, and retraining. A
publicly available real-life data set from semiconductor
manufacturing provided by the University of California Irvine
[33] was used for the initial training. In the use case, instances
are classified as pass or fail based on sensor data collected
during the process. The deployed ML model application
includes prediction, monitoring, and retraining functionality.
Wrapped into a Flask backend, the model returns predictions
based on requests of data in JSON format. Furthermore,
available model versions can be retrieved, monitoring data in
form performance metrics or a confusion matrix can be
calculated, and new model versions can be trained on request.
Using the software Postman, it is possible to make requests to
the application’s endpoints and visualize responses. Especially
the practical implementation could ensure the guideline’s
applicability for the user.

In this paper, a guideline for deploying ML models in
production
environments
was
developed.
It
supports
companies to fully reap the benefits of modeling efforts by
deploying built models into production and using their results,
where they create added value. Given the importance of
ensuring successful deployments,
a clearly structured
methodology was lacking. Therefore, the deployment was
broken down into separate guideline components. For each
component, relevant decisions and steps were provided in a
high-level, generally valid form. Based on this, it was described
in detail how introduced decisions and steps can be applied to
predictive quality under consideration of individual needs and
restrictions of any specific use case.
Limitations exist regarding the area of application. The
guideline
was
conceptualized
for
predictive
quality
applications in production. In its current form, it cannot address
all deviating scenarios with different requirements towards the
deployment. In this context, the validation was also conducted
with a limited scope.
Due to the relevance of deployment across industries, future
lines of investigation include an analysis of how to adjust the
guideline to further use cases within or beyond the
manufacturing industry. Furthermore, the integration of
DevOps techniques into the ML life cycle with the ultimate
goal of automating the whole end-to-end ML pipeline from
data integration to deployment is part of future research.
